topicData.title: Chennai
topicData.subtitle: City · Tamil Nadu, India
topicData.summary: Chennai, capital of Tamil Nadu, was known as Madras during British colonial rule from 1639 until 1996. The city was built around Fort St. George established by the British East India Company and served as the capital of the Madras Presidency.
topicData.aiInsight: The Madras-to-Chennai rename in 1996 was one of India's earliest post-colonial city renames, preceding Mumbai (1995) by a year. The name 'Chennai' derives from Chennapatnam, named after a local Nayak ruler Damarla Chennappa.

nameChanges.name: Madaraspatnam
nameChanges.context: A small fishing village of the Madras people before British arrival.

nameChanges.name: Fort St. George Settlement
nameChanges.meaning: Fort named after patron saint of England
nameChanges.context: British East India Company established Fort St. George in 1644.

nameChanges.name: Madras
nameChanges.context: Capital of Madras Presidency; major British administrative centre.

nameChanges.name: Chennai
nameChanges.context: Tamil Nadu government officially renamed the city on 17 July 1996.

historicalContext.content: Region inhabited by Coromandel Coast fishing communities. Part of Vijayanagara Empire territory.
Francis Day and Andrew Cogan obtained land from local Nayak rulers. Fort St. George became the first British outpost in India.
Capital of Madras Presidency, one of four main British administrative divisions of India. Major port for cotton and textiles.
State capital of Madras State, later Tamil Nadu (1969). Major industrial and cultural hub of South India.
Renamed Chennai in 1996. Now a major IT corridor, automobile manufacturing hub, and home to the 'Detroit of India'.

comments.author: Ananya Krishnan
comments.text: As a Chennai native, the history of our city's name is something every resident should know.

topicData.title: Myanmar
topicData.subtitle: Country · Southeast Asia
topicData.summary: Myanmar, formerly known as Burma, is a Southeast Asian nation with a rich history of kingdoms. The military junta renamed it Myanmar in 1989, a change still not recognised by some countries including the USA.
topicData.aiInsight: The Burma-Myanmar controversy is unique in modern naming history — two names are in active simultaneous use by different governments. Myanmar is the literary/formal Burmese name while Burma derives from the colloquial Bamar pronunciation.

nameChanges.name: Brahmadesh
nameChanges.context: Early Sanskrit references to the region.

nameChanges.name: Pagan Empire
nameChanges.context: First unified Burmese kingdom.

nameChanges.name: Burma
nameChanges.context: British annexed in three wars (1824, 1852, 1885). Named after the Bamar majority ethnic group.

nameChanges.name: Myanmar
nameChanges.meaning: Literary form of Bamar — strong/fast nation
nameChanges.context: Military junta renamed in 1989. Not recognised by USA, UK, and many others.

historicalContext.content: Mon, Pyu, and Bamar peoples established early city-states.
First unified Burmese kingdom; Theravada Buddhism established; thousands of temples built in Bagan.
Three Anglo-Burmese wars resulted in full annexation by 1885. Part of British India until 1937, then separate colony.
Independence in 1948. Military coup in 1962. Renamed Myanmar in 1989. Democratic elections in 2015, military coup again in 2021.

topicData.title: Ho Chi Minh City
topicData.subtitle: City · Southern Vietnam
topicData.summary: Ho Chi Minh City, formerly Saigon, is Vietnam's largest city and economic capital. It was renamed in 1975 after the fall of South Vietnam and the reunification of the country under communist rule.
topicData.aiInsight: The Saigon-to-Ho Chi Minh City rename is politically loaded — locals still call it Saigon in daily speech, and even official postal addresses sometimes use Saigon. It represents one of the most contested modern city renames globally.

nameChanges.name: Prey Nokor
nameChanges.context: Khmer fishing village under Cambodian rule.

nameChanges.name: Saigon
nameChanges.meaning: Possibly from Khmer Prey Nokor or 'Sai Gon' cotton forest
nameChanges.context: Nguyen Lords settled. French made it capital of Cochinchine colony in 1859.

nameChanges.name: Capital of Republic of Vietnam
nameChanges.context: Capital of US-backed South Vietnam during the Vietnam War.

nameChanges.name: Ho Chi Minh City
nameChanges.meaning: Named after Communist leader Ho Chi Minh
nameChanges.context: Renamed after fall of Saigon on 30 April 1975. Locals still use 'Saigon' colloquially.

historicalContext.content: Prey Nokor was a small Khmer fishing settlement at the Mekong delta.
Vietnamese Nguyen Lords settled the region. French captured it in 1859 and made it capital of Cochinchine.
Capital of US-backed South Vietnam. Major conflict zone. Fell to North Vietnamese forces on 30 April 1975.
Reunified under Communist rule. Today Vietnam's economic powerhouse and startup hub.

comments.author: Minh Tran
comments.text: We still call it Saigon. The name lives on in our hearts and streets.

topicData.title: Zimbabwe
topicData.subtitle: Country · Southern Africa
topicData.summary: Zimbabwe was named Rhodesia after British imperialist Cecil Rhodes. After independence, it was briefly Zimbabwe Rhodesia, then became Zimbabwe in 1980, named after the ancient Great Zimbabwe stone structures.
topicData.aiInsight: Zimbabwe is one of few nations named after an ancient archaeological site. 'Zimbabwe' derives from 'dzimba dza mabwe' (Shona for houses of stone), referencing the 900-year-old Great Zimbabwe ruins.

nameChanges.name: Matabeleland / Mashonaland
nameChanges.context: Territories of Ndebele and Shona kingdoms.

nameChanges.name: Rhodesia / Southern Rhodesia
nameChanges.context: British South Africa Company territory, named after Cecil Rhodes.

nameChanges.name: Zimbabwe Rhodesia
nameChanges.context: Brief transitional name after internal settlement.

nameChanges.name: Zimbabwe
nameChanges.meaning: Dzimba dza mabwe — Houses of Stone
nameChanges.context: Independence declared 18 April 1980 under Robert Mugabe.

historicalContext.content: Powerful Shona kingdom built massive stone enclosures — Great Zimbabwe — the largest stone structures in sub-Saharan Africa.
Cecil Rhodes' BSAC colonised the territory. Named Rhodesia after him.
Unilateral Declaration of Independence by Ian Smith. Guerrilla war by ZANU and ZAPU liberation movements.
Independence under Mugabe (1980–2017). Economic crisis in 2000s. Emmerson Mnangagwa took power in 2017 coup.

topicData.title: Jakarta
topicData.subtitle: City · Java, Indonesia
topicData.summary: Jakarta has carried many names through Hindu, Islamic, Portuguese, Dutch, and Japanese colonial periods. Originally Sunda Kalapa, it became Batavia under Dutch rule for 300 years before being renamed Jakarta by the Japanese in 1942.
topicData.aiInsight: Jakarta's naming history mirrors Indonesia's colonial layers perfectly — each ruler renamed the city in their own language and tradition. The Dutch name 'Batavia' survived longest (320 years), while the Hindu name Sunda Kalapa (port city) is the most evocative.

nameChanges.name: Sunda Kalapa
nameChanges.context: Major port of Sunda Kingdom; traded with China, India, and Arabia.

nameChanges.name: Jayakarta
nameChanges.context: Fatahillah renamed it after defeating Portuguese in 1527.

nameChanges.name: Batavia
nameChanges.meaning: Named after ancient Batavian tribe of Netherlands
nameChanges.context: Jan Pieterszoon Coen established Dutch East India Company headquarters here.

nameChanges.name: Jakarta
nameChanges.context: Japanese occupation forces renamed Batavia to Jakarta in 1942. Retained at independence.

historicalContext.content: Major trading port on Java's northwest coast. Traded spices, cloth, and pepper with Asian and Arab merchants.
Fatahillah of Banten Sultanate defeated Portuguese and named it Jayakarta.
VOC headquarters for 320 years. Centre of Dutch East Indies spice trade empire.
Capital of independent Indonesia (1945). Currently being replaced as capital by Nusantara (new capital in Borneo, planned 2024).

topicData.title: Harare
topicData.subtitle: Capital City · Zimbabwe
topicData.summary: Harare was named Salisbury by British settlers in 1890, after the British Prime Minister Lord Salisbury. Upon Zimbabwe's independence in 1980, it was renamed Harare, derived from the Shona chief Neharawa.
topicData.aiInsight: The Salisbury-to-Harare rename is emblematic of Zimbabwe's broader decolonisation — the capital city, like the country itself, shed its colonial name for a pre-colonial Shona identity.

nameChanges.name: Harare Kraal
nameChanges.context: Shona settlement of chief Neharawa, meaning 'he who does not sleep'.

nameChanges.name: Fort Salisbury
nameChanges.context: Pioneer Column of Cecil Rhodes planted the British flag here on 12 September 1890.

nameChanges.name: Salisbury
nameChanges.context: Capital of Southern Rhodesia, Rhodesia, and briefly Zimbabwe Rhodesia.

nameChanges.name: Harare
nameChanges.meaning: From Neharawa — he who does not sleep
nameChanges.context: Renamed two years after independence to honour pre-colonial Shona heritage.

historicalContext.content: Shona chieftaincy settlement on the Mashonaland plateau.
Fort established by Cecil Rhodes' Pioneer Column. Capital of Southern Rhodesia from 1923.
Capital of Rhodesia; centre of white minority rule until independence.
Renamed Harare in 1982. Economic decline in 2000s. Recovery attempts ongoing.

topicData.title: Thailand
topicData.subtitle: Country · Southeast Asia
topicData.summary: Thailand was known as Siam for centuries before being renamed in 1939 by Prime Minister Phibun Songkhram. The name 'Thailand' means 'Land of the Free' — notably the only Southeast Asian country never colonised by Europeans.
topicData.aiInsight: The Siam-to-Thailand rename in 1939 was a nationalist statement — 'Muang Thai' (Land of Thai people) emphasised ethnic Thai identity during a period of rising pan-Thai nationalism and Japanese regional influence.

nameChanges.name: Suvarnabhumi
nameChanges.context: Ancient Sanskrit/Indian name for the region.

nameChanges.name: Sukhothai Kingdom
nameChanges.context: First major Thai kingdom.

nameChanges.name: Siam
nameChanges.context: Used by Ayutthaya and Chakri dynasty. Recognised internationally by Western powers.

nameChanges.name: Thailand
nameChanges.context: PM Phibun renamed it on 23 June 1939 to assert Thai ethnic identity.

historicalContext.content: First Thai kingdom. King Ramkhamhaeng created the Thai alphabet.
Ayutthaya became one of Asia's largest and most cosmopolitan cities before Burmese destruction in 1767.
Chakri dynasty established Bangkok as capital. International treaties signed as Siam.
Never colonised. Constitutional monarchy since 1932. Major Southeast Asian economic power.

topicData.title: Oslo
topicData.subtitle: Capital City · Norway
topicData.summary: Oslo, Norway's capital, was renamed Christiania (later Kristiania) in 1624 by King Christian IV of Denmark-Norway after a devastating fire. It reverted to the original Norse name Oslo in 1925.
topicData.aiInsight: Oslo's naming history reflects Norwegian national identity struggles under Danish then Swedish rule. The 1925 reversion to Oslo was a deliberate act of cultural reclamation following Norway's full independence in 1905.

nameChanges.name: Ánslo
nameChanges.context: Founded by King Harald Hardrada. Major trading port.

nameChanges.name: Christiania
nameChanges.context: After great fire of 1624, rebuilt and renamed by Danish King Christian IV.

nameChanges.name: Kristiania
nameChanges.context: Respelled to Norwegian form. City under Swedish union until 1905 independence.

nameChanges.name: Oslo
nameChanges.context: Restored original Norse name on 1 January 1925, 20 years after independence.

historicalContext.content: Founded by King Harald Hardrada. Became episcopal see and trade hub.
Under Kalmar Union then Danish rule. Renamed Christiania after 1624 fire.
Under Swedish crown after Napoleonic Wars. Kristiania became modernised capital.
Full independence 1905. Renamed Oslo 1925. Occupied by Nazis 1940–45. Modern Scandinavian capital.

topicData.title: Bangladesh
topicData.subtitle: Country · South Asia
topicData.summary: Bangladesh, meaning 'Country of Bengal', was East Pakistan from 1947 to 1971. After a bloody Liberation War, it declared independence as Bangladesh on 26 March 1971, with the name proposed by Sheikh Mujibur Rahman.
topicData.aiInsight: Bangladesh's naming combines 'Bangla' (Bengali language/people) with 'desh' (country/homeland). The choice explicitly rejected Pakistani identity and embraced the Bengali language that was the very cause of the Liberation War — sparked by attempts to impose Urdu.

nameChanges.name: Bengal (Banga)
nameChanges.context: Ancient Bengal region. Part of Maurya, Gupta, and Pala empires. British Bengal Presidency.

nameChanges.name: East Bengal
nameChanges.context: British partitioned Bengal in 1905 (reversed 1911). Then East Bengal at independence.

nameChanges.name: East Pakistan
nameChanges.context: Majority Bengali population oppressed, especially Bengali language. Language Movement 1952.

nameChanges.name: Bangladesh
nameChanges.context: Independence declared 26 March 1971 after Liberation War. Recognised internationally December 1971.

historicalContext.content: One of the wealthiest regions in ancient Asia. Pala Empire (750–1120 CE) was a major Buddhist power.
Part of Mughal Bengal, then British India. Major jute and cotton producer.
Pakistan attempted to impose Urdu. Bengali speakers protested. Police fired on protesters on 21 February 1952 — now International Mother Language Day.
Brutal crackdown by Pakistani military. 9-month war with Indian support. Independence achieved 16 December 1971.

topicData.title: Kyoto
topicData.subtitle: City · Kinki Region, Japan
topicData.summary: Kyoto was Japan's imperial capital for over 1,000 years. Originally named Heiankyō (Capital of Peace), it was later called Miyako (The Capital) before being known simply as Kyoto — meaning 'capital city' in Japanese.
topicData.aiInsight: The word 'Kyoto' literally means 'capital city' in Sino-Japanese — so when Tokyo became the new capital in 1869, Kyoto became a city whose very name means 'capital' but is no longer one. Tokyo (Eastern Capital) was named in deliberate contrast.

nameChanges.name: Yamashiro Province
nameChanges.context: Ancient province before the capital was moved here.

nameChanges.name: Heiankyō
nameChanges.context: Emperor Kanmu moved capital from Nara to Heiankyō in 794.

nameChanges.name: Miyako
nameChanges.context: Common name used in literature and daily speech to mean THE capital city.

nameChanges.name: Kyoto
nameChanges.context: After Emperor Meiji moved court to Tokyo in 1869, this name stuck as the formal city name.

historicalContext.content: Imperial court flourished. The Tale of Genji written here. Height of classical Japanese culture.
Power shifted to samurai warlords while emperors remained in Kyoto. Onin War devastated the city in 1467.
Tokugawa shogunate ruled from Edo (Tokyo) but emperor stayed in Kyoto. Preserved as cultural capital.
Lost capital status in 1869 to Tokyo. Preserved from WWII bombing. UNESCO heritage temples and traditions intact.

comments.author: Hiroshi Tanaka
comments.text: Kyoto meaning 'capital' yet not being the capital is one of history's great ironies.

topicData.title: Eswatini
topicData.subtitle: Country · Southern Africa
topicData.summary: Eswatini, formerly Swaziland, is one of the world's last absolute monarchies. King Mswati III renamed it Eswatini in 2018 on the 50th anniversary of independence, meaning 'Land of the Swazis' in the native SiSwati language.
topicData.aiInsight: The 2018 Swaziland-to-Eswatini rename is the most recent major national rename. King Mswati III argued 'Swaziland' confused foreigners who confused it with Switzerland. 'Eswatini' directly translates to 'Land of the Swazis' in SiSwati.

nameChanges.name: Ngwane Kingdom
nameChanges.context: Founded by King Ngwane III. Consolidated under King Mswati I.

nameChanges.name: Swaziland
nameChanges.context: British protectorate established 1903 after Anglo-Boer War.

nameChanges.name: Kingdom of Swaziland
nameChanges.context: Independence from Britain on 6 September 1968.

nameChanges.name: Eswatini
nameChanges.context: King Mswati III renamed on 50th independence anniversary, 19 April 2018.

historicalContext.content: Swazi people consolidated under strong kings. Resisted Zulu expansion.
Mineral rights given to foreign companies. Administered as separate territory from South Africa.
Independence under King Sobhuza II (then Mswati III). Constitutional monarchy abolished in 1973 to become absolute monarchy.
Renamed in 2018. Last absolute monarchy in Africa. HIV/AIDS crisis and pro-democracy protests.

topicData.title: Czechia
topicData.subtitle: Country · Central Europe
topicData.summary: Czechia (Czech Republic) has a complex naming history spanning Bohemia, Moravia, Czechoslovakia, and the modern Czech Republic. The country adopted the short-form name Czechia in 2016, registered with the United Nations.
topicData.aiInsight: The Bohemia-Czechia evolution shows how a country's name can outlive the empire that coined it. 'Bohemia' (from Celtic Boii tribe) was used for 1,000+ years before the Slavic 'Čechy' became the modern Czech/Czechia.

nameChanges.name: Boiohaemum (Bohemia)
nameChanges.context: Celtic Boii tribe inhabited the region. Roman name Boiohaemum.

nameChanges.name: Duchy/Kingdom of Bohemia
nameChanges.context: Přemyslid dynasty founded. Part of Holy Roman Empire then Habsburg Austria.

nameChanges.name: Czechoslovakia
nameChanges.context: Founded after WWI collapse of Austria-Hungary. Split into two nations in 1993 Velvet Divorce.

nameChanges.name: Czech Republic / Czechia
nameChanges.context: Peaceful separation from Slovakia on 1 January 1993. Short name Czechia registered at UN in 2016.

historicalContext.content: Přemyslid and Luxembourg dynasties. King Charles IV made Prague a European capital of learning.
Under Habsburg Austria. Hussite religious wars preceded Protestant-Catholic conflicts.
One of central Europe's most democratic states between the wars. Nazi occupation 1938–45. Communist rule 1948–89. Velvet Revolution 1989.
Peaceful Velvet Divorce from Slovakia in 1993. EU member 2004. Short name Czechia adopted 2016.

topicData.title: Peking Duck
topicData.subtitle: Dish · Chinese Imperial Cuisine
topicData.summary: Peking Duck is one of China's most celebrated imperial dishes, documented in imperial court records since the Yuan Dynasty (1271–1368). Its name directly reflects the historical naming of Beijing — 'Peking' being the old Western romanisation.
topicData.aiInsight: Peking Duck uniquely preserves the old name 'Peking' even after Beijing's official renaming in the 1980s — making it a culinary time capsule. Renaming it 'Beijing Duck' has been discussed but met with resistance as the old name carries authenticity.

nameChanges.name: Shāo Yāzi (Roasted Duck)
nameChanges.context: First documented in Hu Sihui's 'Proper and Essential Things for the Emperor's Food and Drink' (1330).

nameChanges.name: Menlu Duck
nameChanges.context: Refined for imperial banquets in the Forbidden City.

nameChanges.name: Peking Duck
nameChanges.meaning: Duck from Peking (old romanisation of Beijing)
nameChanges.context: First restaurant Quanjude opened in 1864. Name spread internationally as Western trade grew.

nameChanges.name: Běijīng Kǎoyā
nameChanges.context: Official Chinese name post-Pinyin standardisation. Western menus still use 'Peking Duck'.

historicalContext.content: Developed in Yuan and Ming imperial kitchens. Special breeds of ducks fed grain for 45 days to achieve desired fat layer.
Dish became the centrepiece of imperial banquets. Quanjude restaurant opened 1864 serving the public.
Survived political upheavals. Mao Zedong famously served it to foreign dignitaries.
Peking Duck restaurants now worldwide. UNESCO submitted for cultural heritage consideration.

comments.author: Wei Chen
comments.text: The fact that we still call it Peking Duck shows how food names outlast political renames.

topicData.title: Vindaloo
topicData.subtitle: Dish · Goan-Portuguese Fusion Cuisine
topicData.summary: Vindaloo is a spicy Goan curry whose name derives from the Portuguese dish 'carne de vinha d'alhos' (meat in wine and garlic). Brought by Portuguese colonisers to Goa in the 16th century, it was transformed by local Goan cooks into the fiery curry known today.
topicData.aiInsight: Vindaloo is a perfect example of culinary colonialism inverted — a Portuguese dish transformed beyond recognition by Indian spices and chilli peppers (themselves a New World ingredient). The name is Portuguese, the dish is entirely Goan.

nameChanges.name: Carne de Vinha d'Alhos
nameChanges.context: Portuguese sailors preserved pork in wine vinegar and garlic for long sea voyages.

nameChanges.name: Vindahos / Vindalo
nameChanges.context: Local Goans adapted the dish, replacing wine with palm vinegar and adding Indian spices.

nameChanges.name: Vindaloo
nameChanges.context: British popularised the Anglicised spelling. Now synonymous with extreme heat in British-Indian cuisine.

historicalContext.content: Portuguese established trading posts. Local Goan cooks blended European and Indian culinary traditions uniquely.
India annexed Goa in 1961. Goan cuisine — including Vindaloo — spread globally via the Indian diaspora and British-Indian restaurants.
British curry houses took Vindaloo and made it the benchmark for heat. The 'Vindaloo challenge' became a cultural phenomenon in UK.

topicData.title: Croissant
topicData.subtitle: Pastry · Viennese / French Cuisine
topicData.summary: The croissant's ancestor, the Kipferl, originated in Vienna, Austria. French bakers transformed it into the laminated, buttery crescent pastry in the early 19th century. The name 'croissant' is French for 'crescent', referencing its iconic shape.
topicData.aiInsight: The croissant shows how a food migrates both geographically and culturally — Austria invented the shape, France reinvented the recipe, and Britain and America adopted both. The crescent shape itself may reference the Ottoman crescent moon, symbolising Christian victory over the Ottomans at Vienna (1683).

nameChanges.name: Kipferl
nameChanges.context: Crescent-shaped Austrian roll. Legend says bakers heard Ottoman tunnelling under Vienna (1683) and alerted soldiers.

nameChanges.name: Croissant de Vienne
nameChanges.context: Austrian officer August Zang opened Boulangerie Viennoise in Paris in 1838. French bakers adapted with laminated dough.

nameChanges.name: Croissant
nameChanges.context: Standardised worldwide as the flaky, laminated pastry. French culinary exports spread it globally.

historicalContext.content: Battle of Vienna (1683) legend: bakers alerted sleeping city to Ottoman tunnelling. Crescent pastry made to celebrate victory over the Ottoman Empire.
August Zang brought Viennese baking to Paris. French bakers added laminated (feuilletée) dough technique to create the modern croissant.
Became symbol of French cuisine worldwide despite Austrian origins. Industrial production began in 1900s. Now eaten on every continent.

comments.author: Marie Dupont
comments.text: As a French person I was shocked to learn croissants are Austrian in origin!

topicData.title: Chai
topicData.subtitle: Beverage · Indian Spiced Tea
topicData.founded: 1830s CE (British introduction of tea to India)
topicData.summary: Chai simply means 'tea' in Hindi (from the Chinese 'chá'). The spiced version — masala chai — was developed when the British introduced tea cultivation to India in the 1830s, blending it with indigenous spices like cardamom, ginger, and cinnamon.
topicData.aiInsight: The word 'chai' is found across dozens of languages in various forms: chai (Hindi), chay (Russian/Turkish), tè (Italian/Spanish), tea (English) — all derived from the Chinese 'chá' or the Amoy dialect 'te'. 'Chai tea' in Western cafes is a redundancy literally meaning 'tea tea'.

nameChanges.name: Chá (茶)
nameChanges.context: Emperor Shennong legend. Tea cultivation in Yunnan province.

nameChanges.name: Chai / Chay
nameChanges.context: Spread via Silk Road trade routes. Persian and Turkic traders adopted 'chay'.

nameChanges.name: Masala Chai
nameChanges.context: British planted tea in Assam. Indian vendors added local spices — cardamom, ginger, cinnamon, cloves — creating masala chai.

nameChanges.name: Chai / Chai Latte
nameChanges.context: Starbucks popularised 'chai latte' in 1990s. Now globally sold as a sweet, milky drink.

historicalContext.content: Tea drinking developed in China. Spread along Silk Road from 600 CE.
British planted Assam tea to break Chinese monopoly. Indian street vendors (chaiwallahs) created masala chai blends.
Western coffee chains popularised chai lattes. 'Chai tea latte' is technically a triple redundancy.

quickFacts.label: Spices used
quickFacts.value: Cardamom, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper

topicData.title: Mango
topicData.subtitle: Fruit · Tropical · South Asian Origin
topicData.summary: The mango's English name comes from the Tamil 'māṅkāy' via Portuguese 'manga'. Native to South Asia, mangoes have been cultivated for over 4,000 years and appear in Sanskrit literature, Buddhist scriptures, and Mughal imperial records.
topicData.aiInsight: The mango's linguistic journey — Tamil māṅkāy → Portuguese manga → English mango — mirrors the spice trade routes. In Sanskrit it is 'āmra', in Hindi 'aam', in Tamil 'māngo'. It is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines simultaneously.

nameChanges.name: Āmra (आम्र)
nameChanges.context: Cultivated in Indian subcontinent for 4,000+ years. Mentioned in Vedic texts.

nameChanges.name: Māṅkāy
nameChanges.context: Tamil word for the unripe mango fruit specifically.

nameChanges.name: Manga
nameChanges.context: Vasco da Gama arrived in Kerala (1498). Portuguese adopted māṅṅa as manga and spread it to Africa and Brazil.

nameChanges.name: Mango
nameChanges.context: First English use c.1582. Now the universal name used globally.

historicalContext.content: Cultivated in South Asia for millennia. Lord Buddha gifted a mango grove. Mentioned in Ramayana and Mahabharata.
Emperor Akbar planted 100,000 mango trees in Darbhanga. Mughal miniatures show mangoes prominently.
Portuguese spread mango to West Africa and Brazil (1700s). British introduced to Caribbean.
Now grown in 100+ countries. India produces 40% of world's mangoes. 1,500+ named varieties.

topicData.title: Ramen
topicData.subtitle: Dish · Japanese Noodle Soup
topicData.summary: Ramen is a Japanese noodle soup dish with Chinese origins. The word derives from Chinese 'la mian' (pulled noodles). Introduced to Japan by Chinese immigrants in the late 19th century, it evolved into a distinct Japanese cuisine during the post-WWII period.
topicData.aiInsight: Ramen is a study in culinary nationalism — a Chinese dish adopted and reinvented by Japan to become one of the world's most recognised Japanese foods. Post-WWII American wheat surplus and Japanese government policy shaped ramen's evolution into street food staple.

nameChanges.name: Lā Miàn (拉麵)
nameChanges.context: Chinese pulled wheat noodles. Technique of stretching dough into long thin noodles.

nameChanges.name: Shina Soba
nameChanges.context: Chinese immigrants sold noodle soup in Yokohama Chinatown. Term 'Shina' is now offensive.

nameChanges.name: Ramen
nameChanges.meaning: Pulled/stretched noodle (Japanese pronunciation of lā miàn)
nameChanges.context: Post-WWII American wheat aid. Government promoted wheat noodles. Instant ramen invented 1958 by Nissin's Momofuku Ando.

historicalContext.content: Lā Miàn (pulled noodles) is a traditional Chinese technique. Brought to Japan by Chinese immigrants.
Sold as Shina Soba in street stalls. Regional variations developed.
American wheat aid made flour cheap. Instant ramen invented 1958. Ramen stalls proliferated nationwide.
Instant ramen became world's most consumed food. Gourmet ramen restaurants emerged. Over 6 billion servings/year.

topicData.title: Pizza
topicData.subtitle: Dish · Neapolitan / Italian Cuisine
topicData.summary: The word 'pizza' first appeared in a Latin text in Gaeta, Italy in 997 CE. While flatbreads predate pizza by thousands of years, the modern tomato-based pizza was developed in Naples in the 18th century after tomatoes arrived from the Americas.
topicData.aiInsight: Pizza's etymology remains debated — theories range from Lombardic 'pizzo' (mouthful) to Latin 'pinsere' (to stamp/flatten). The tomato pizza we know is only 200–300 years old, yet flatbreads with toppings are documented in Ancient Egypt, Greece, and Persia.

nameChanges.name: Plakous / Flatbread
nameChanges.context: Ancient Greeks and Romans ate focaccia-like flatbreads with oil, cheese, and herbs.

nameChanges.name: Picea / Piza
nameChanges.context: First written record: a Gaeta notary document (997 CE) requiring delivery of 'duodecim pizze' (twelve pizzas).

nameChanges.name: Pizza Napolitana
nameChanges.context: Tomatoes (from Americas) adopted by Neapolitan poor in 1700s. Pizza Margherita created for Queen Margherita in 1889.

nameChanges.name: Pizza
nameChanges.context: Italian immigrants took pizza to America. New York and Chicago styles developed. Now a $140 billion global industry.

historicalContext.content: Flatbreads with toppings eaten across Mediterranean. Roman soldieers ate panis focacius.
Naples was a densely populated, poor city. Street food vendors (pizzaioli) sold cheap flatbreads.
South American tomatoes (initially feared as poisonous) adopted by Neapolitan poor. Pizza became a cheap meal.
Italian immigrants brought pizza to New York. NY slice, deep dish Chicago, and California styles developed. Now world's most popular food.

comments.author: Giovanni Russo
comments.text: Pizza is from Naples, not Rome. Most people don't know true Neapolitan pizza history!

topicData.title: Chocolate
topicData.subtitle: Food · Mesoamerican Origin
topicData.summary: Chocolate derives from the Nahuatl (Aztec) word 'xocolātl', meaning 'bitter water'. Consumed as a frothy, bitter, spiced drink by the Maya and Aztec for 3,000 years before Spanish conquistadors brought it to Europe, where sugar transformed it into the sweet we know today.
topicData.aiInsight: Xocolātl → chocolatl → chocolat → chocolate: the name migrated through Spanish, French, and English while the flavour profile flipped entirely — from bitter/spiced Mesoamerican beverage to sweet European confection. The Aztec word 'cacahuatl' gave us both cacao and cocoa.

nameChanges.name: Kakaw (𝒌𝒂𝒌𝒂𝒘)
nameChanges.context: Olmec and Maya cultivated cacao trees. Used in rituals and as currency.

nameChanges.name: Xocolātl
nameChanges.context: Aztec emperor Montezuma consumed vast amounts. Used as currency and ceremonial drink.
nameChanges.source: Aztec Codices & Bernal Díaz del Castillo, 1521

nameChanges.name: Chocolatl / Chocolate
nameChanges.context: Hernán Cortés brought cacao to Spain in 1528. Added sugar, cinnamon. Became an elite European drink.

nameChanges.name: Chocolate (solid)
nameChanges.context: Joseph Fry made first solid chocolate bar (1847). Cadbury, Nestlé, Hershey industrialised production.

historicalContext.content: Sacred drink in Mayan and Aztec cultures. Cacao beans used as currency. Only elite consumed it.
Spanish added sugar and cinnamon; European royalty developed addiction. Chocolate houses rivalled coffeehouses.
Solid chocolate bar invented 1847 (Fry), milk chocolate 1875 (Nestlé/Peter), conching improved texture.
$130+ billion global industry. West Africa supplies 70% of cacao. Child labour concerns ongoing.

topicData.title: Naan
topicData.subtitle: Bread · Central Asian / South Asian
topicData.summary: Naan comes from the Persian word for bread ('nān'). First documented in Delhi in 1300 CE, it spread across South Asia through Mughal courts. The Persian word 'nān' was generic for any bread — it became specifically associated with the leavened tandoor-baked flatbread of South Asia.
topicData.aiInsight: Saying 'naan bread' is a linguistic redundancy — 'naan' already means 'bread' in Persian. This pattern (where a borrowed food word gets 'bread'/'soup'/'tea' appended by target language speakers) is common: chai tea, hoi sin sauce, shrimp scampi.

nameChanges.name: Nān (نان)
nameChanges.context: Persian 'nān' meant any type of flatbread — not specifically the South Asian tandoor bread.

nameChanges.name: Naan (Delhi Court)
nameChanges.context: First written reference in Amir Khusrau's 1300 CE work describing bread at Delhi Sultanate court.

nameChanges.name: Naan / Nan
nameChanges.context: British colonialists adopted it. Multiple spellings — naan, nan — used internationally.

historicalContext.content: The word nān is found across Persian, Pashto, Kurdish, and Uyghur — all meaning some form of flatbread.
Mughal emperors made naan a royal food. Tandoor ovens (from Central Asia) spread across South Asia.
British took the recipe home. Garlic naan, cheese naan, and peshwari naan are British-Indian inventions.

topicData.title: Ketchup
topicData.subtitle: Condiment · Global
topicData.summary: Ketchup has nothing to do with tomatoes in its origin. The word derives from the Chinese Hokkien dialect 'kê-tsiap', a fermented fish sauce. British sailors brought it from Southeast Asia, and it evolved through many forms before Heinz introduced tomato ketchup in 1876.
topicData.aiInsight: Original ketchup was a fish sauce — unrecognisable from today's tomato version. The transformation from salty/fermented to sweet/tomato took 200 years and multiple countries. The word 'ketchup', 'catchup', and 'catsup' are all in use — Heinz uses 'ketchup', Lea & Perrins uses 'ketchup', but some southern US brands still say 'catsup'.

nameChanges.name: Kê-tsiap / Kecap
nameChanges.meaning: Brine of pickled fish or shellfish
nameChanges.context: Fermented fish sauce used in Fujian province and throughout Southeast Asia.

nameChanges.name: Catsup / Catchup
nameChanges.context: British sailors brought Southeast Asian fish sauce home. Early British recipes used anchovies, mushrooms, walnuts — no tomatoes.

nameChanges.name: Tomato Ketchup
nameChanges.context: First tomato ketchup recipe published by James Mease in 1812. Heinz standardised it in 1876.

nameChanges.name: Ketchup
nameChanges.context: Heinz's recipe and glass bottle standardised 'ketchup' globally. 650 million bottles sold annually.

historicalContext.content: Kê-tsiap was a pungent fermented fish sauce used across Southeast Asia, especially in China's Fujian province.
British adapted the concept using local ingredients — mushroom ketchup, walnut ketchup, oyster ketchup. No tomatoes.
Americans added tomatoes (previously feared as poisonous). Heinz introduced preservative-free tomato ketchup in 1876.
Heinz dominates globally with 60% market share. Now used on every continent.

comments.author: Tom Richards
comments.text: Mind-blowing that ketchup started as a fish sauce! The tomato version is less than 200 years old.

topicData.title: Google
topicData.subtitle: Company · Technology / Search Engine
topicData.summary: Google began as a Stanford research project called BackRub in 1996. Renamed Google in 1997, the name is a play on 'googol' (10¹⁰⁰) — a mathematical term chosen to represent the company's mission to organise the vast quantity of information on the web.
topicData.aiInsight: The name 'Google' was reportedly a misspelling of 'googol' when Larry Page and Sergey Brin were checking domain availability. The error has become one of the most valuable brand names in history, worth over $2 trillion. The word 'google' is now a verb in the Oxford English Dictionary.

nameChanges.name: BackRub
nameChanges.context: Larry Page and Sergey Brin's Stanford research project that ranked web pages by back-link analysis.

nameChanges.name: Google (googol misspelling)
nameChanges.context: Named Google in September 1997. Company incorporated 4 September 1998. Became world's most used search engine.

nameChanges.name: Alphabet (parent company)
nameChanges.meaning: Collection of letters / vast range of companies
nameChanges.context: Google restructured under Alphabet Inc in October 2015. Google remains a subsidiary.

historicalContext.content: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met at Stanford. BackRub → Google. Incorporated in a Menlo Park garage.
Survived dot-com crash. IPO in 2004. Acquired YouTube (2006), Android (2005), DoubleClick (2008).
Android became world's most used mobile OS. Google Chrome, Gmail, Maps became essential global services.
Restructured as Alphabet. Ventures in AI (DeepMind), healthcare (Verily), autonomous vehicles (Waymo).

comments.author: Dev Patel
comments.text: BackRub is such an awkward name for a search engine. Google was clearly an upgrade!

topicData.title: Amazon
topicData.subtitle: Company · E-commerce / Technology
topicData.summary: Amazon was originally called Cadabra, Inc. when Jeff Bezos founded it in 1994. He renamed it Amazon in 1995 because A) Amazon River is the world's largest river (implying scale), B) it starts with 'A' (top of alphabetical lists), and C) Cadabra sounded like 'cadaver'.
topicData.aiInsight: Jeff Bezos reportedly considered over 300 names including Relentless.com (which still redirects to Amazon.com). 'Amazon' was chosen deliberately for its associations with massive scale and because search engines ranked sites alphabetically at the time.

nameChanges.name: Cadabra, Inc.
nameChanges.context: Jeff Bezos' original name. His lawyer misheard it as 'cadaver'. Changed before launch.

nameChanges.name: Amazon.com
nameChanges.meaning: World's largest river — implying the world's largest store
nameChanges.context: Launched as online bookstore 16 July 1995. Expanded to 'everything store'. World's largest e-commerce company.

historicalContext.content: Bezos left Wall Street job, drove to Seattle. Online bookstore launched from his garage.
Stock crashed 90% in dot-com bust. Survived by expanding categories and building infrastructure.
Amazon Web Services (2006) became foundation of cloud internet. Kindle revolutionised reading.
Prime membership 200M+. AWS powers Netflix, NASA, CIA. Bezos became world's richest person.

topicData.title: Pepsi
topicData.subtitle: Brand · Beverage / PepsiCo
topicData.summary: Pepsi began as 'Brad's Drink' invented by pharmacist Caleb Bradham in 1893. Renamed Pepsi-Cola in 1898, the name combines 'pepsin' (digestive enzyme) and 'kola nuts'. It went bankrupt twice before becoming the global brand that challenges Coca-Cola.
topicData.aiInsight: Pepsi's naming history reflects early soft drink marketing — many were sold as health tonics (Coca-Cola had cocaine, Pepsi claimed digestive benefits via 'pepsin'). The Pepsi-Cola Company went bankrupt in 1923 and 1931 before stabilising, showing how naming/branding alone couldn't save a struggling company.

nameChanges.name: Brad's Drink
nameChanges.context: Pharmacist Caleb Bradham mixed water, sugar, caramel, lemon oil, kola nuts in New Bern, NC.

nameChanges.name: Pepsi-Cola
nameChanges.context: Bradham renamed it Pepsi-Cola in 1898. Trademarked 1903. Bankrupted 1923 and 1931.

nameChanges.name: Pepsi
nameChanges.context: Name shortened to Pepsi in 1961. Parent company became PepsiCo in 1965 (merger with Frito-Lay).

historicalContext.content: Sold as a health tonic with digestive claims. Bradham lost the company in 1923 sugar speculation.
Bought for $35,000 in 1931 bankruptcy. Survived by selling 12oz bottles for 5¢ (same price as Coke's 6oz).
'Pepsi Generation' youth marketing campaign. Michael Jackson's famous Pepsi commercial 1984. Pepsi Challenge blind tests.
Diversified into snacks (Doritos, Lay's). Mountain Dew, Gatorade, Lipton added. Revenue now $91B+.

topicData.title: Nike
topicData.subtitle: Brand · Athletic Footwear & Apparel
topicData.summary: Nike was originally Blue Ribbon Sports, founded by Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman in 1964 as a US distributor of Japanese Onitsuka Tiger shoes. Renamed Nike in 1971 — after the Greek goddess of victory — just before launching its own shoe line.
topicData.aiInsight: The Nike name was famously suggested by Jeff Johnson (first employee) the night before trademark filing, reportedly from a dream. The Swoosh logo was designed by Carolyn Davidson for $35. Phil Knight reportedly said 'I don't love it, but it'll grow on me' — it became the world's most recognised logo.

nameChanges.name: Blue Ribbon Sports
nameChanges.context: Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman distributed Onitsuka Tiger shoes from Japan. Operated from the back of a car.

nameChanges.name: Nike, Inc.
nameChanges.context: Name suggested by Jeff Johnson. Launched with the Swoosh logo (designed for $35). Just Do It tagline added 1988.

historicalContext.content: Knight sold shoes from his car at track meets. Bowerman experimented with shoe designs using a waffle iron.
Own shoe line launched. Air technology introduced 1979 (Nike Air). Michael Jordan signed 1984 — Air Jordan line born.
'Just Do It' campaign 1988. Tiger Woods partnership 1996. $51B revenue today. World's most valuable apparel brand.

topicData.title: Starbucks
topicData.subtitle: Brand · Coffee Chain
topicData.summary: Starbucks was named after Starbuck, the first mate in Herman Melville's Moby Dick. The founders initially considered 'Cargo House' and 'Pequod' (the ship in Moby Dick) before settling on Starbucks. The name evokes the seafaring romance of early coffee traders.
topicData.aiInsight: The Starbucks name is a literary homage — Starbuck in Moby Dick is calm, rational, and represents restraint against Captain Ahab's obsession. Whether intentional or not, naming a coffee company after a character known for calm seems ironic given caffeine's stimulating effects.

nameChanges.name: Cargo House (proposed)
nameChanges.context: One of several names considered by founders Baldwin, Bowker, and Siegl.

nameChanges.name: Pequod (proposed)
nameChanges.context: Gordon Bowker proposed Pequod (Moby Dick's ship). Partner rejected it — 'No one will drink a cup of Pee-quod!'

nameChanges.name: Starbucks Coffee, Tea and Spice
nameChanges.meaning: Named after Starbuck, first mate in Moby Dick
nameChanges.context: Original store sold coffee beans and equipment. Howard Schultz joined in 1982 and transformed into espresso bar.

nameChanges.name: Starbucks Corporation
nameChanges.context: Howard Schultz bought company in 1987, renamed Starbucks Corp. Expanded globally from 1996.

historicalContext.content: Single store in Pike Place Market selling coffee beans and equipment. Howard Schultz inspired by Italian espresso bars.
Schultz bought company, expanded concept. 1,000 stores by 2000. International expansion began 1996.
35,000+ locations worldwide. Introduced Frappuccino, PSL (Pumpkin Spice Latte). $36B revenue.

topicData.title: LEGO
topicData.subtitle: Brand · Toy Company · Denmark
topicData.summary: LEGO is derived from the Danish 'leg godt', meaning 'play well'. Founded by Ole Kirk Christiansen in 1932 as a woodworking company, it accidentally became the meaning of 'connect' in Latin (lego = I connect/assemble), which LEGO embraces as a secondary meaning.
topicData.aiInsight: LEGO is a rare case of a company name that works perfectly in two languages accidentally — 'leg godt' (play well) in Danish and 'lego' (I connect) in Latin. Ole Kirk Christiansen reportedly discovered the Latin connection after naming it. The interlocking brick wasn't invented until 1958.

nameChanges.name: Ole Kirk's Billund Carpentry
nameChanges.context: Ole Kirk Christiansen made wooden furniture and household items in Billund, Denmark.

nameChanges.name: LEGO (wooden toys)
nameChanges.context: Switched to toys during Great Depression. LEGO name coined 1934. Won award for best Danish toy 1948.

nameChanges.name: LEGO Group (plastic bricks)
nameChanges.context: Automatic Binding Bricks launched 1949. Renamed LEGO Bricks 1953. Stud-and-tube system patented 1958.

historicalContext.content: Carpenter Ole Kirk made wooden toys when furniture orders dried up. Company survived on toys.
First plastic bricks 1949. Interlocking brick system patented 1958 — the design still used today.
Legoland opened 1968. Castle, Space, City, Technic themes launched. Near-bankruptcy in 2003.
Turned around from bankruptcy. Lego movies (2014+), video games, Ideas platform. World's largest toy company by revenue.

topicData.title: Nokia
topicData.subtitle: Company · Finnish Technology
topicData.summary: Nokia was founded in 1865 as a pulp mill on the Nokianvirta river in Finland. It diversified through rubber boots, cables, and television before pivoting to mobile phones in the 1990s, becoming the world's top mobile manufacturer before losing dominance to Apple and Android.
topicData.aiInsight: Nokia is the ultimate example of a company whose product range has nothing to do with its name — a river-derived Finnish name now synonymous globally with mobile phones, though Nokia itself has exited the phone hardware business. The Nokia brand is now licensed to HMD Global, a separate Finnish company.

nameChanges.name: Nokia Aktiebolag (Nokia Ab)
nameChanges.context: Pulp mill founded by Fredrik Idestam. 'Nokia' is a town in Finland named after the river.

nameChanges.name: Nokia Corporation
nameChanges.context: Finnish Rubber Works and Finnish Cable Works merged with Nokia Ab in 1967. Mobile division launched 1980s.

historicalContext.content: Paper, rubber boots, cables, and later televisions. Very little to do with technology.
First car phone 1982. 1100 model sold 250 million units — most sold phone ever. Dominant 40% global market share in 2000.
iPhone launched 2007. Nokia couldn't adapt. Microsoft acquired mobile division for $7.2B in 2014.
Nokia Networks became major 5G infrastructure provider. HMD Global licensed Nokia brand for phones.

topicData.title: Intel
topicData.subtitle: Company · Semiconductor / Technology
topicData.summary: Intel was founded by Gordon Moore and Robert Noyce in 1968 as NM Electronics. The name was changed to Intel — a portmanteau of 'Integrated Electronics' — just days after incorporation. Intel had to buy the rights to the name from a motel chain.
topicData.aiInsight: Intel had to buy its own name — 'Intel' was trademarked by Intelco (a hotel chain) when they wanted it. They paid to acquire it. The name 'Intel' is both an abbreviation of 'Integrated Electronics' and the word for intelligence, giving it a dual meaning that proved prescient for the AI era.

nameChanges.name: NM Electronics
nameChanges.context: Gordon Moore (M) and Robert Noyce (N) — name from their initials. Used for just a few days.

nameChanges.name: Intel Corporation
nameChanges.context: Name coined by Noyce and Moore. Had to buy it from a hotel chain. First product: 3101 SRAM chip (1969).

historicalContext.content: Founded by Moore and Noyce (who left Fairchild Semiconductor). Memory chips first product. Andy Grove joined as employee #3.
4004 first commercial microprocessor (1971). IBM PC used Intel 8088 (1981). Moore's Law — transistors double every 2 years.
Pentium brand launched 1993. 'Intel Inside' campaign. World's most valuable semiconductor company.
ARM architecture challenged Intel in mobile. TSMC overtook in manufacturing. Major AI chip investments.

topicData.title: Sony
topicData.subtitle: Company · Electronics / Entertainment
topicData.summary: Sony began as Tokyo Tsushin Kogyo (Tokyo Telecommunications Engineering Corporation) in 1946. Renamed Sony in 1958 — a combination of 'sonus' (Latin for sound) and 'sonny' (American slang for a bright young person), chosen to be easily pronounceable in all languages.
topicData.aiInsight: Sony's founders Masaru Ibuka and Akio Morita specifically chose 'Sony' because it had no meaning in any language except as a brand — making it equally neutral globally. The deliberate choice of a meaningless-but-friendly word was revolutionary for Japanese companies of the era.

nameChanges.name: Tokyo Tsushin Kogyo K.K.
nameChanges.context: Founded in a bombed-out department store by Masaru Ibuka and Akio Morita with 8 employees and $530 capital.

nameChanges.name: Sony Corporation
nameChanges.context: Renamed for US market listing. First transistor radio 1955. Walkman 1979. PlayStation 1994.

historicalContext.content: Started repairing radios in bombed-out Tokyo. First major product: tape recorder (1950), Japan's first transistor radio (1955).
Trinitron TV (1968), Betamax (1975 — lost format war), Walkman (1979 — revolutionary), CD co-developed with Philips (1982).
PlayStation launched 1994. Acquired Columbia Pictures, CBS Records. Now spans electronics, entertainment, gaming, music.

topicData.title: Adidas
topicData.subtitle: Brand · German Sportswear
topicData.summary: Adidas was founded by Adolf 'Adi' Dassler in 1949, after splitting from the company he ran with his brother Rudolf (who founded Puma). The name Adidas is derived from Adi Dassler's nickname and surname, and has been mistakenly expanded as 'All Day I Dream About Sport/Soccer'.
topicData.aiInsight: The Dassler brothers' split is one of business history's most bitter family feuds — their rivalry divided the entire town of Herzogenaurach into Adidas and Puma factions for decades. 'Adidas' is simply 'Adi' + 'Das(sler)' — the folk acronym 'All Day I Dream About Sport' is a backronym invented by fans.

nameChanges.name: Gebrüder Dassler Schuhfabrik
nameChanges.context: Rudolf and Adolf Dassler ran a family shoe workshop. Made shoes for Jesse Owens at 1936 Berlin Olympics.

nameChanges.name: Adidas AG
nameChanges.context: After bitter split with brother Rudolf, Adi founded Adidas on 18 August 1949. Rudolf founded Puma across town.

historicalContext.content: Brothers made shoes worn by Jesse Owens at 1936 Olympics. WWII caused tensions leading to bitter personal split.
Adidas dominated football. Supplied shoes at 1954 World Cup (Germany won — credited partly to Adidas studs).
Muhammad Ali, Pele, Maradona wore Adidas. Stan Smith tennis shoes became iconic fashion item.
Yeezy collaboration (Kanye West). Superstar shoes in streetwear. $24B revenue. #2 global sportswear brand.

topicData.title: X (formerly Twitter)
topicData.subtitle: Platform · Social Media
topicData.summary: Twitter was founded in 2006 and named after 'a short burst of inconsequential information' — the co-founders considered the name Twitch before settling on Twitter. Elon Musk acquired it in 2022 and rebranded to X, discarding one of the world's most recognisable brand names overnight.
topicData.aiInsight: The Twitter-to-X rebrand is history's most dramatic destruction of brand equity — Twitter had 16 years of brand recognition and $4-5 billion in brand value. 'X' is Musk's obsession (X.com, SpaceX, Tesla Model X, xAI). Branding experts called it the worst rebrand since New Coke.

nameChanges.name: Twitch (proposed)
nameChanges.context: Co-founder Jack Dorsey considered Twitch before Twitter. Twitch name was later used by Amazon's gaming platform.

nameChanges.name: Twitter
nameChanges.meaning: A short burst of inconsequential information / bird chirping
nameChanges.context: Launched 21 March 2006. IPO 2013. 'Tweeting', 'tweet', 'Twitter' entered global vocabulary.

nameChanges.name: X
nameChanges.context: Elon Musk acquired Twitter for $44B in October 2022. Rebranded to X on 23 July 2023. Blue bird logo replaced.

historicalContext.content: Launched as SMS-inspired microblogging. Arab Spring, political movements, #MeToo shaped on Twitter. 450M daily users.
$44B acquisition completed 27 October 2022. Mass layoffs (80% staff). Verification system overhauled.
Rebranded to X on 23 July 2023. Logo changed. 'Everything app' ambitions — payments, video, messaging.

comments.author: Sarah Kim
comments.text: The Twitter→X rebrand threw away billions in brand value overnight. Textbook example of what not to do.

topicData.title: Neem Tree
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Azadirachta indica · South Asia
topicData.summary: The Neem tree, known as Azadirachta indica in scientific Latin, has been called 'the village pharmacy' in India. Its name derives from the Persian 'nīm' (half) and Sanskrit 'nimba', and was known to ancient Indians as the divine tree of Sarva Roga Nivarini — the cure for all ailments.
topicData.aiInsight: Neem's naming history spans Sanskrit, Persian, Arabic, and modern scientific Latin, each reflecting a different culture's use. The scientific name Azadirachta comes from Persian 'azad darakht-i Hind' (free tree of India) — as the tree was free/wild and gave freely to all who used it.

nameChanges.name: Nimba (निम्ब)
nameChanges.context: Mentioned in Atharva Veda and Ayurvedic texts as medicinal and sacred.

nameChanges.name: Azad Darakht / Nīm
nameChanges.context: Persian traders and Mughal court botanists named it for its widespread free availability.
nameChanges.source: Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine, 1025 CE

nameChanges.name: Margosa / Indian Lilac
nameChanges.context: British colonial botany recorded it as Margosa tree for its bitter compounds.
nameChanges.source: British East India Company Botanical Records

nameChanges.name: Azadirachta indica
nameChanges.context: Formally classified by A.H.L. de Jussieu in 1830. Used globally in organic pesticides, medicine, and cosmetics.

historicalContext.content: All parts used in Ayurveda: leaves, bark, seeds, oil. Called Sarva Roga Nivarini (all-disease remover).
Used in traditional medicine across South Asia. Mughal gardens featured neem prominently.
British studied its properties. Neem oil used as insecticide. Twigs used as toothbrushes across India.
W.R. Grace Company tried to patent neem-based pesticides in 1990s — India fought and won the patent battle (2000), calling it biopiracy.

topicData.title: Cannabis
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Cannabis sativa · Central Asia
topicData.summary: Cannabis has one of the longest cultivation histories of any plant — over 10,000 years. The plant has been called hemp, marijuana, ganja, hashish, weed, and hundreds of regional names. Its naming history reflects its diverse uses: medicine, spirituality, textiles, and recreation.
topicData.aiInsight: The word 'marijuana' was deliberately popularised in the US in the 1930s by anti-cannabis campaigners who wanted to associate the plant with Mexican immigrants (marijuana/marihuana being a Mexican Spanish term). Before this, Americans primarily knew it as hemp or cannabis.

nameChanges.name: Bhanga / Bhang (भांग)
nameChanges.context: One of five sacred plants in Vedas. Used in religious rituals. Lord Shiva associated with cannabis.

nameChanges.name: Kannabis
nameChanges.context: Herodotus described Scythians inhaling cannabis vapour. Word root of all modern European terms.

nameChanges.name: Hashish / Ganja
nameChanges.meaning: Hashish = dry grass; Ganja = Sanskrit for hemp
nameChanges.context: Arab traders spread hashish. Marco Polo's 'Assassins' legend linked to hashish (disputed).

nameChanges.name: Marijuana / Cannabis
nameChanges.context: Marijuana term popularised in US 1930s prohibition campaigns. 'Cannabis' now preferred in medical contexts.

historicalContext.content: One of first cultivated plants. Hemp fibres for rope and cloth. Medical and spiritual use across Asia.
Arabic traders spread hash across Middle East and North Africa. European sailors used hemp ropes.
US banned cannabis 1937. UN Convention on Narcotic Drugs 1961 pushed global prohibition.
Counterculture movements. Medical legalisation from 1990s. Recreational legal in Canada (2018), many US states, and growing list of countries.

topicData.title: Tulsi
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Holy Basil · Ocimum tenuiflorum
topicData.summary: Tulsi, known in English as Holy Basil, is one of Hinduism's most sacred plants. The Sanskrit name 'Tulsi' means 'the incomparable one'. It has been cultivated and worshipped in India for over 5,000 years and features prominently in Ayurveda.
topicData.aiInsight: Tulsi's scientific name 'Ocimum tenuiflorum' contains both Greek and Latin roots — Greek 'okimon' (basil) and Latin 'tenuiflorum' (with fine/small flowers). The common name 'Holy Basil' was given by European botanists but doesn't capture the depth of religious reverence Tulsi holds — it's worshipped as a goddess, not merely a herb.

nameChanges.name: Tulasi / Tulsi (तुलसी)
nameChanges.context: Sacred to Vishnu. Every Hindu home traditionally has a Tulsi plant. Used in pujas and Ayurvedic medicine.

nameChanges.name: Ocimum sanctum
nameChanges.context: Linnaeus classified it as Ocimum sanctum in 1753 (now a synonym).

nameChanges.name: Holy Basil / Ocimum tenuiflorum
nameChanges.context: Current accepted scientific name. 'Holy Basil' widely used in Western herbal medicine and wellness industry.

historicalContext.content: Considered a manifestation of goddess Lakshmi. Every Hindu home grows Tulsi plant. Used in all Hindu rituals.
Extensively documented in Charaka Samhita for respiratory, digestive, and immune conditions.
Western wellness industry adopted Tulsi as 'adaptogen'. Tulsi tea widely sold globally.

quickFacts.label: Religious status
quickFacts.value: Sacred to Vishnu / worshipped as deity

topicData.title: Banyan Tree
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Ficus benghalensis · South Asia
topicData.summary: The Banyan tree gets its English name from 'Banyans' — Hindu traders (Vanias) who conducted business and rested under these large trees. Portuguese traders observed this practice and named the tree after its users. Its Sanskrit name 'Vata Vriksha' and scientific name Ficus benghalensis have entirely different origins.
topicData.aiInsight: The English name 'banyan' is unique in botany — it's named after the people who used it (Hindu traders/banias), not the plant itself. This is the reverse of most plant naming conventions. The tree is India's national tree and the world's largest tree by canopy area.

nameChanges.name: Vata Vriksha (वट वृक्ष)
nameChanges.context: Sacred tree in Hindu mythology. Under a banyan tree, Buddha received enlightenment according to some traditions. Goddess Savitri associated with banyan.

nameChanges.name: Banyan / Banian
nameChanges.meaning: Named after Bania (Hindu merchant caste) traders
nameChanges.context: Portuguese observed Bania traders resting and conducting business under large trees. Transferred the merchant's name to the tree.

nameChanges.name: Ficus benghalensis
nameChanges.context: Linnaeus classified as Ficus benghalensis in 1753. National Tree of India.

historicalContext.content: One of Hinduism's most sacred trees. Associated with immortality and longevity due to its aerial roots.
Hindu traders (banias/vanias) used these trees as meeting points and shade for commerce.
Portuguese/British botanists noted the trader connection and named it 'Banyan tree'.

topicData.title: Ashwagandha
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Withania somnifera · Adaptogen
topicData.summary: Ashwagandha is one of Ayurveda's most important medicinal plants. Its Sanskrit name means 'smell of a horse' — referring to both its distinctive odour and its traditional use to impart the strength and vitality of a horse. The scientific name Withania somnifera references its sleep-inducing properties.
topicData.aiInsight: The contrast between Ashwagandha's Sanskrit name (horse smell/strength) and its scientific Latin name (sleep-inducing Withania) reveals how two cultures noticed completely different properties of the same plant. The Western wellness industry rebranded it as 'Indian Ginseng' — a misleading name as it's not related to ginseng at all.

nameChanges.name: Ashwagandha (अश्वगंधा)
nameChanges.context: Used in Ayurvedic medicine for stress, vitality, and male fertility for 3,000+ years.

nameChanges.name: Withania somnifera
nameChanges.context: Classified by C.H. Schultes. Named for its calming, sleep-supporting properties.

nameChanges.name: Indian Ginseng / Ashwagandha
nameChanges.context: Western supplement industry branded it 'Indian Ginseng'. Despite no relation to ginseng, the name boosted sales. Now used in $1B+ wellness category.

historicalContext.content: Core Rasayana (rejuvenating) herb in Ayurveda. Used for immunity, stress, male vitality.
British botanists classified it. Used in colonial Indian medicine.
Adaptogens trend in Western wellness. Ashwagandha sales increased 500%+ in 2020s. Clinical studies on cortisol reduction.

topicData.title: Bamboo
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Bambusoideae · Asia & Tropics
topicData.summary: The word 'bamboo' entered English from Dutch 'bamboe', borrowed from Malay 'bambu', which likely came from the Kannada or Sanskrit term. Despite being called a tree, bamboo is technically a grass — the world's fastest-growing plant.
topicData.aiInsight: Bamboo's name traces the colonial spice trade route — Malay → Portuguese/Dutch → English. The Sanskrit root 'vamsha' (bamboo/lineage) gives us a philosophically rich connection: bamboo's hollow stem and straight growth made it a metaphor for spiritual lineage in Hindu and Buddhist texts.

nameChanges.name: Vamsha (वंश)
nameChanges.context: Sanskrit term used both for the plant and metaphorically for family/genealogical lineage.

nameChanges.name: Bambu (bambu/bamb)
nameChanges.context: Malay 'bambu'. Some theories say it's onomatopoeic — bamboo explodes loudly when burned.

nameChanges.name: Bamboe / Bamboo
nameChanges.context: Dutch navigator Jan Huyghen van Linschoten introduced 'bamboe' to European languages (1598).

historicalContext.content: Used for construction, food, tools, music, writing surfaces, and weapons for millennia across Asia.
Europeans introduced to bamboo via Southeast Asian trade. Kew Gardens cultivated specimens.
Bamboo promoted as sustainable building material. Fastest growing plant (91cm/day). Used in flooring, clothing, and paper.

topicData.title: Eucalyptus
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Myrtaceae · Australia
topicData.summary: Eucalyptus was formally named in 1789 by French botanist Charles-Louis L'Héritier de Brutelle. The name derives from Greek 'eu' (well) and 'calyptos' (covered), referring to the cap that covers the flower bud. Indigenous Australians had dozens of names for different species.
topicData.aiInsight: The eucalyptus naming illustrates colonial botanical erasure — dozens of Aboriginal Australian names for different species were replaced with a single Greek-derived scientific classification. For example, the Yolŋu people called their local eucalyptus by specific names based on seasonal and ceremonial use.

nameChanges.name: Various Aboriginal Names
nameChanges.meaning: Varied by species and use (Jarrah, Karri, Marri, etc.)
nameChanges.context: Aboriginal Australians had species-specific names for dozens of eucalyptus types, each reflecting its properties and uses.

nameChanges.name: Eucalyptus
nameChanges.context: L'Héritier de Brutelle named it from a specimen collected during Captain Cook's voyage. Genus has 700+ species.

historicalContext.content: Used for food, medicine, tool-making, and in fire management. Specific species had specific ceremonial roles.
Joseph Banks brought specimens on Cook's 1770 voyage. Botanist L'Héritier formally classified in 1789.
Planted worldwide for timber, oil, and paper. Australia's iconic Blue Mountains haze is eucalyptus oil vapour.

topicData.title: Coffee Plant
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Coffea arabica · Ethiopia
topicData.summary: Coffee is named after the Kaffa region of Ethiopia where it was first discovered. From the Ethiopian highlands, it spread to Yemen (14th century) where it was first brewed as a drink, then to Ottoman Turkey, and finally to Europe via Venetian merchants.
topicData.aiInsight: The word coffee traces an extraordinary global journey: Kaffa (Ethiopia) → qahwa (Arabic, meaning 'that which prevents sleep') → kahve (Turkish) → caffè (Italian) → coffee (English). In Ethiopia itself, the drink is called 'buna' — not derived from the European coffee chain of names at all.

nameChanges.name: Buna (ቡና)
nameChanges.context: Legend: Kaldi the goat herder noticed goats energised by red berries in Kaffa region, Ethiopia.

nameChanges.name: Qahwa (قهوة)
nameChanges.meaning: That which prevents sleep / a type of wine
nameChanges.context: Yemeni Sufi monks brewed coffee to stay awake for night prayers. Qahwa spread across Muslim world.

nameChanges.name: Kahve
nameChanges.context: First coffeehouses (kahvehane) opened in Constantinople 1555. Coffee became social institution.

nameChanges.name: Coffee / Caffè
nameChanges.meaning: From Turkish kahve via Italian caffè
nameChanges.context: Venetian merchants brought coffee to Italy around 1600. 'Coffee' appeared in English by 1600.

historicalContext.content: Wild coffee plants in Kaffa. Berries chewed for energy. Not yet brewed as drink.
Yemen first brewed coffee. Ottoman coffeehouses became centres of intellectual life — banned twice for stimulating dissent.
Coffeehouses opened in Oxford (1650), London (1652), Paris (1672). Lloyd's of London started in a coffeehouse.
Industrial roasting and instant coffee. Starbucks (1971) globalised coffee culture. $500B+ global industry.

topicData.title: Lotus
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Nelumbo nucifera · Sacred Flower of Asia
topicData.summary: The lotus holds the distinction of being sacred to three world religions simultaneously — Hinduism, Buddhism, and ancient Egyptian religion. Its name varies significantly across cultures: Padma in Sanskrit, Nilufar in Persian, Lotus in Greek — each tradition seeing different symbolism in the same flower.
topicData.aiInsight: The lotus has the remarkable botanic property of emerging clean from muddy water — a self-cleaning mechanism now studied by nanotechnology researchers as the 'Lotus Effect'. This physical property inspired its spiritual symbolism of purity arising from impurity across three independent civilisations.

nameChanges.name: Seshen (ꜣb)
nameChanges.context: Sacred blue lotus of Egypt (actually Nymphaea caerulea). Used in religious ceremonies, mummification.

nameChanges.name: Padma (पद्म) / Kamala
nameChanges.context: National flower of India. Symbol of Brahma, Vishnu, Lakshmi, Saraswati. Core Buddhist symbol.

nameChanges.name: Lotos
nameChanges.context: Homer's Odyssey mentions 'lotus-eaters'. Greek term spread through Mediterranean.

nameChanges.name: Nelumbo nucifera
nameChanges.context: Linnaeus classified from Sinhalese (Sri Lanka) term. National flower of India and Vietnam.

historicalContext.content: Blue lotus sacred to Ra and Osiris. Used in ceremonies, depicted in hieroglyphics and tomb paintings.
Central symbol in both religions. Deities sit or stand on lotus thrones. Represents spiritual awakening.
Lotus Effect (self-cleaning) discovered 1970s by Wilhelm Barthlott. Applied to nanotechnology surfaces.

topicData.title: Aloe Vera
topicData.subtitle: Plant · Aloe barbadensis miller · Succulent
topicData.summary: Aloe vera's name is a combination of Arabic 'alloeh' (bitter, shining substance) and Latin 'vera' (true/genuine), reflecting its identification as the true medicinal aloe among related species. Used in medicine and cosmetics for over 6,000 years, it appears in Egyptian, Greek, Indian, and Chinese ancient records.
topicData.aiInsight: The name 'Aloe vera' contains an Arabic-Latin hybrid that developed during the Middle Ages when Arab scholars were the primary transmitters of classical Greek medical knowledge to Europe. The 'vera' (true) distinguishes it from many other aloe species, indicating Europeans specifically wanted this species for medicine.

nameChanges.name: Alloeh / Aloe (علو)
nameChanges.context: Used in Egyptian Ebers Papyrus (1550 BCE). Cleopatra reputedly used aloe gel.

nameChanges.name: Aloe (Αλόη)
nameChanges.context: Dioscorides documented aloe in De Materia Medica (77 CE) for wound healing.

nameChanges.name: Aloe vera
nameChanges.context: Linnaeus named it Aloe perfoliata var. vera (1753). Current name Aloe barbadensis miller is technically correct but Aloe vera is universally used.

historicalContext.content: Used in Egypt, India, China, Greece. Alexander the Great reportedly secured island of Socotra for aloe supply for his armies.
Spanish and Portuguese spread aloe to Americas. Caribbean aloe 'barbadensis' named after Barbados.
Aloe vera gel became staple in cosmetics. $2B+ global market. In products from sunscreens to juices.

topicData.title: Atom
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Physics / Chemistry
topicData.summary: The word 'atom' comes from the Ancient Greek 'atomos' (ατομος) meaning 'indivisible' or 'uncuttable'. Ironically, the 20th century proved atoms are very much divisible — into protons, neutrons, electrons, and further into quarks. The name has survived despite being scientifically incorrect.
topicData.aiInsight: The atom is one of science's great naming ironies — called 'indivisible' in Greek, it was split in 1932. We've kept the wrong name out of historical habit. The subatomic particles (proton, neutron, electron, quark) that actually proved atoms are divisible all have their own interesting etymologies.

nameChanges.name: Atomos (ἄτομος)
nameChanges.context: Democritus and Leucippus proposed all matter consists of tiny indivisible particles called atomos.

nameChanges.name: Atomus (Latin)
nameChanges.context: Lucretius used atomus in De Rerum Natura. Concept adopted in medieval alchemy and natural philosophy.
nameChanges.source: Lucretius, De Rerum Natura, c.55 BCE

nameChanges.name: Atom (chemical element)
nameChanges.meaning: Smallest unit of a chemical element
nameChanges.context: John Dalton formalised atomic theory in 1808 — each element made of indivisible atoms (still believed).
nameChanges.source: John Dalton, A New System of Chemical Philosophy, 1808

nameChanges.name: Atom (divisible)
nameChanges.meaning: Nuclear particle made of protons, neutrons, electrons
nameChanges.context: Electron discovered 1897, nucleus 1911, proton 1920, neutron 1932. Atoms proved divisible — name retained.

historicalContext.content: Democritus, Leucippus proposed atomic theory philosophically — without experimental evidence.
Dalton used atomic theory to explain chemical reactions. Set relative atomic masses.
Electron, proton, neutron discovered. Bohr model. Quantum mechanics developed. Nuclear fission achieved 1938.
Quarks discovered (1968+). Standard Model of particle physics. Higgs boson found 2012.

topicData.title: Vitamin
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Biochemistry / Nutrition
topicData.summary: The word 'vitamin' was coined in 1912 by Polish-American biochemist Casimir Funk from Latin 'vita' (life) and 'amine' (nitrogen compound). Ironically, most vitamins are NOT amines — but the name stuck. The alphabetical naming system (A, B, C...) also reflects our sequential discovery history.
topicData.aiInsight: Vitamin naming is a masterclass in scientific naming gone awry. 'Vitamin' contains 'amine' because Funk thought they were all amines — most aren't. The B-vitamins jumped from B1 to B2 to B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12 — skipping numbers because some 'B vitamins' turned out to not be vitamins at all.

nameChanges.name: Accessory food factors
nameChanges.meaning: Unknown essential trace elements in food
nameChanges.context: Frederick Hopkins described unknown essential factors in food in 1906 Nobel Prize research.

nameChanges.name: Vitamine
nameChanges.context: Casimir Funk coined 'vitamine' in 1912. Believed all essential nutrients were amines.
nameChanges.source: Casimir Funk, Journal of State Medicine, 1912

nameChanges.name: Vitamin (dropped 'e')
nameChanges.meaning: Life factor (shortened, 'e' dropped when amines theory disproved)
nameChanges.context: Jack Cecil Drummond proposed dropping 'e' in 1920 when fat-soluble vitamins proved NOT to be amines.

historicalContext.content: Scurvy, rickets, beriberi linked to diet but cause unknown. James Lind's citrus cure for scurvy (1747).
Vitamins A, B, C, D discovered in sequence. Beriberi linked to B1 deficiency.
Vitamins synthesised industrially. Supplementation became standard. $43B global vitamin industry.

topicData.title: Dinosaur
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Palaeontology
topicData.summary: The word 'dinosaur' was coined in 1842 by British palaeontologist Richard Owen from Greek 'deinos' (terrible, powerful) and 'sauros' (lizard). Owen invented the term to classify a group of large extinct reptiles that were being discovered across Europe and North America.
topicData.aiInsight: Dinosaurs are not actually lizards — the 'sauros' (lizard) in the name is taxonomically incorrect. Birds are the only living dinosaurs (from theropod lineage), yet we call them birds, not dinosaurs. Meanwhile, the species once considered most stereotypically dinosaurian (T. rex, Stegosaurus) are probably more accurate — it's the 'lizard' part that's wrong.

nameChanges.name: Dragon / Great Lizard (folk)
nameChanges.context: Dinosaur bones likely inspired dragon myths across cultures — China, Europe, Greece all found large fossil bones.

nameChanges.name: Dinosauria
nameChanges.context: Richard Owen coined the term in 1842 to group Megalosaurus, Iguanodon, and Hylaeosaurus together.
nameChanges.source: Richard Owen, Report on British Fossil Reptiles, 1842

historicalContext.content: Megalosaurus described 1824 (Buckland), Iguanodon 1825 (Mantell). Owen grouped them as Dinosauria.
Bone Wars between Marsh and Cope. Major North American finds. T. rex named 1905.
Robert Bakker argued dinosaurs were warm-blooded. Feathered dinosaur fossils discovered 1990s. Bird-dinosaur link confirmed.

topicData.title: Coronavirus
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Virology
topicData.summary: The name 'coronavirus' was coined in 1968 by virologist June Almeida who first visualised the virus using electron microscopy. The name comes from Latin 'corona' (crown) because the spike proteins surrounding the virus resemble a solar corona or crown. COVID-19 is caused by SARS-CoV-2.
topicData.aiInsight: June Almeida — who first identified and photographed the coronavirus in 1966 — was rejected when she first submitted her paper because reviewers thought she was simply looking at bad influenza images. The naming honours her observation that the spike proteins create a crown-like appearance visible in electron microscopy.

nameChanges.name: Novel respiratory virus (unnamed)
nameChanges.context: June Almeida at St Thomas' Hospital London identified a novel virus structure in 1966.
nameChanges.source: Almeida & Tyrrell, Journal of General Virology, 1966

nameChanges.name: Coronavirus
nameChanges.context: International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses named the family Coronaviridae in 1968.

nameChanges.name: SARS-CoV-2 / COVID-19
nameChanges.context: WHO named disease COVID-19 and virus SARS-CoV-2 on 11 February 2020 to avoid geographic stigmatisation.

historicalContext.content: June Almeida used electron microscopy to visualise the distinctive spike-protein crown. Named coronaviruses 1968.
SARS-CoV-1 killed 800+ people. First major coronavirus pandemic scare.
SARS-CoV-2 emerged in late 2019. Declared pandemic March 2020. 7+ million deaths globally.

quickFacts.label: Spike proteins
quickFacts.value: Give crown appearance under electron microscope

comments.author: Dr. Priya Nair
comments.text: June Almeida's discovery story is remarkable — she was initially dismissed before naming the most consequential virus family.

topicData.title: Black Hole
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Astrophysics
topicData.summary: The term 'black hole' was coined by physicist John Wheeler in 1967 during a lecture. Before Wheeler's catchy term, these objects were called 'gravitationally completely collapsed stars' or 'dark stars'. The name elegantly captures the physics — no light can escape (black) from a spherical region (hole).
topicData.aiInsight: John Wheeler famously said a journalist suggested 'black hole' during one of his lectures in 1967 and he immediately adopted it. Before this, a three-page description ('gravitationally completely collapsed object') was required. The right name can transform scientific communication — 'black hole' became instantly memorable and spread globally within years.

nameChanges.name: Dark Star
nameChanges.meaning: Star so dense light cannot escape
nameChanges.context: John Michell (1783) and Pierre-Simon Laplace (1796) proposed massive objects where escape velocity exceeds light speed.

nameChanges.name: Schwarzschild Singularity
nameChanges.meaning: Named after Karl Schwarzschild who solved Einstein's equations
nameChanges.context: Einstein's 1915 General Relativity predicted such objects. Schwarzschild calculated the radius in 1916.

nameChanges.name: Frozen Star
nameChanges.meaning: Soviet physicists' term — time appears frozen at event horizon
nameChanges.context: Soviet term for the same object emphasising the time dilation observed at the event horizon.

nameChanges.name: Black Hole
nameChanges.meaning: Region of spacetime from which nothing, including light, can escape
nameChanges.context: John Archibald Wheeler coined/popularised 'black hole' at 1967 NASA lecture. Became universal scientific term.

historicalContext.content: Michell and Laplace predicted 'dark stars'. Einstein's General Relativity predicted spacetime curvature.
Schwarzschild radius calculated 1916. Oppenheimer & Snyder proved stellar collapse possible (1939).
First black hole candidate Cygnus X-1 (1964). Hawking radiation theory (1974). First image of black hole (M87*, 2019).

topicData.title: Antibiotic
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Medicine / Pharmacology
topicData.summary: The term 'antibiotic' was coined by microbiologist Selman Waksman in 1941, from Greek 'anti' (against) and 'bios' (life). Waksman himself discovered streptomycin, the first cure for tuberculosis. The word replaced older terms like 'antibiosis' and captures the mechanism: one living organism producing compounds that kill another.
topicData.aiInsight: The term 'antibiotic' contains a philosophical irony — it means 'against life', yet antibiotics save millions of lives annually. Waksman coined it specifically for naturally-derived bacterial inhibitors to distinguish them from purely synthetic antimicrobials like sulfonamides.

nameChanges.name: Antibiosis
nameChanges.context: Vuillemin coined 'antibiose' in 1889 to describe one organism inhibiting another.

nameChanges.name: Antibiotic
nameChanges.meaning: Anti (against) + bios (life) — substance from life that fights life
nameChanges.context: Waksman coined 'antibiotic' in 1941. Received Nobel Prize 1952 for streptomycin discovery.

historicalContext.content: Alexander Fleming noticed mould killing bacteria (1928). Howard Florey and Ernst Chain purified penicillin for clinical use (1940).
Streptomycin (1943), Tetracycline (1955), Erythromycin (1952) discovered. Transformative for infectious disease.
Overuse led to resistance. MRSA, drug-resistant TB growing crises. WHO warns of post-antibiotic era.

topicData.title: Galaxy
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Astronomy
topicData.summary: The word 'galaxy' comes from the Ancient Greek 'galaxias' (γαλαξίας), meaning 'milky' — referring to the Milky Way. In Greek mythology, the Milky Way was spilled breast milk from Hera (Zeus's wife). The term now applies to the 2 trillion+ galaxies in the observable universe.
topicData.aiInsight: Every galaxy in the universe is named after milk because ancient Greeks could only see our Milky Way. The scientific word 'galaxy' carries an intimate domestic origin — spilled breast milk — applied to the largest structures in the observable universe.
