'Andromeda Galaxy' meanwhile is named after a mythological princess.

nameChanges.name: Galaxias Kyklos (γαλαξίας κύκλος)
nameChanges.context: Aristotle used the term for the Milky Way band visible in the sky. Milk associated with Hera mythology.

nameChanges.name: Via Lactea
nameChanges.context: Roman Latin equivalent. 'Lactea' gives us lactose, lactate. Still used in some Romance languages.

nameChanges.name: Galaxy (system of stars)
nameChanges.meaning: Extended from Milky Way to all star systems
nameChanges.context: When telescopes revealed other 'nebulae' were separate star systems, 'galaxy' was extended to all of them.

historicalContext.content: Milky Way explained through mythology. Galileo's 1609 telescope revealed it as stars.
Philosophers proposed 'island universes'. Hubble proved Andromeda was a separate galaxy in 1924.
2 trillion galaxies estimated in observable universe. James Webb Space Telescope imaging earliest galaxies.

topicData.title: Quark
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Particle Physics
topicData.summary: Quark is a subatomic particle name taken from James Joyce's novel Finnegans Wake ('Three quarks for Muster Mark!'). Physicist Murray Gell-Mann coined it in 1964 when proposing the quark model. It's one of science's most creatively named particles.
topicData.aiInsight: Quark is the only fundamental physics particle named after a line in an Irish modernist novel. Murray Gell-Mann already had the 'quark' sound in his head (rhyming with 'work') before finding the Joyce quote, and used it to justify his pronunciation. It shows how even physicists can be poets at naming.

nameChanges.name: Aces (proposed)
nameChanges.meaning: Playing card term for top particles
nameChanges.context: George Zweig independently proposed quarks the same year and called them 'aces'. Gell-Mann's 'quark' won out.

nameChanges.name: Quark
nameChanges.meaning: From James Joyce, Finnegans Wake — 'Three quarks for Muster Mark!'
nameChanges.context: Murray Gell-Mann took the sound 'quork' he had in mind and connected it to Joyce's text. Nobel Prize 1969.

historicalContext.content: Hundreds of new particles discovered — the 'particle zoo'. Gell-Mann's Eightfold Way organised them (1961).
Gell-Mann and Zweig independently proposed quarks. Deep inelastic scattering at SLAC confirmed quarks (1968).
Six quarks (up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom). Standard Model of particle physics established.

quickFacts.label: Six types (flavours)
quickFacts.value: Up, Down, Charm, Strange, Top, Bottom

quickFacts.label: Quark confinement
quickFacts.value: Cannot exist alone — always bound in hadrons

topicData.title: Internet
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Computer Science / Network
topicData.summary: The word 'internet' comes from 'internetworking' — the interconnection of multiple computer networks. The US military ARPANET (1969) was its predecessor. The term 'internet' as a noun was popularised in the 1980s, and the World Wide Web (often confused with the internet itself) was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989.
topicData.aiInsight: Most people use 'internet' and 'World Wide Web' interchangeably — but the internet is the global network infrastructure (pipes), while the web is one service running on it (websites). Email, FTP, streaming, and messaging also run on the internet but are not 'the web'. Berners-Lee invented the web; Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn invented the internet protocols.

nameChanges.name: ARPANET
nameChanges.context: US Defense Department network connecting UCLA, Stanford, UC Santa Barbara, and Utah. First message sent 1969.

nameChanges.name: Internetwork / Catenet
nameChanges.context: Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn's TCP/IP protocol paper (1974) used 'internetwork'. Louis Pouzin proposed 'catenet'.

nameChanges.name: Internet
nameChanges.context: RFC 675 (1974) first formal use. Widely adopted 1982 with TCP/IP. Commercial internet opened 1991.

historicalContext.content: ARPANET designed for nuclear-war-survivable communication. First email sent 1971.
TCP/IP protocols standardised. NSFNET connected universities. Over 100,000 hosts by 1989.
Berners-Lee invented WWW 1989, opened 1991. Netscape browser 1994. Google 1998. Smartphones changed internet fundamentally.

comments.author: Alex Morgan
comments.text: The internet vs web distinction is one everyone should understand. Great breakdown here.

topicData.title: DNA
topicData.subtitle: Scientific Term · Molecular Biology / Genetics
topicData.summary: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid) was first discovered by Swiss chemist Friedrich Miescher in 1869 in pus from surgical bandages. It was originally called 'nuclein'. The double helix structure was determined by Watson and Crick in 1953 (building on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray crystallography data).
topicData.aiInsight: DNA's discovery story is one of science's great controversies — Rosalind Franklin's X-ray image 'Photo 51' was shown to Watson without her knowledge by her colleague Wilkins. Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the 1962 Nobel Prize; Franklin had died of cancer in 1958 and Nobel prizes are not awarded posthumously. Her role was publicly unrecognised for decades.

nameChanges.name: Nuclein
nameChanges.context: Friedrich Miescher extracted acidic substance from white blood cell nuclei in surgical pus. Called it nuclein.

nameChanges.name: Nucleic Acid
nameChanges.context: Richard Altmann renamed nuclein as nucleic acid in 1889, recognising its acidic properties.

nameChanges.name: Deoxyribonucleic Acid / DNA
nameChanges.meaning: Deoxy (missing oxygen) + ribose sugar + nucleic acid
nameChanges.context: The 'deoxy' distinguishes DNA from RNA. Watson-Crick double helix structure published 1953.

historicalContext.content: Discovered 1869. Griffith's transformation experiment (1928) suggested DNA carries genetic information.
Avery, MacLeod, McCarty confirmed DNA carries genes (1944). Franklin's X-ray crystallography and Watson-Crick double helix (1953).
Genetic code cracked (1960s). Restriction enzymes (1970s). PCR invented (1983). Human Genome Project (2003). CRISPR gene editing (2012).
